name: Sirius
notable_for: The brightest star in Earth's night sky; part of a binary system with the white dwarf Sirius B; worshipped by the ancient Egyptians as Sopdet and associated with the Nile flood

name: Canopus
notable_for: The second-brightest star in the night sky; used by spacecraft (including Mars rovers) for navigation as a bright south celestial hemisphere reference star

name: Rigil Kentaurus
notable_for: The brightest member of the Alpha Centauri system and the closest star system to the Sun; visually the third-brightest star in the sky; very similar to the Sun in spectral type

name: Arcturus
notable_for: The brightest star in the northern celestial hemisphere; a red giant star; the 1933 World's Fair in Chicago was opened by light from Arcturus activating a photoelectric cell

name: Vega
notable_for: Served as the northern pole star around 12,000 BCE and will again in 13,727 CE; used as a reference star for the definition of the photometric magnitude scale; rapidly rotating oblate star

name: Capella
notable_for: A spectroscopic binary system of two giant G-type stars orbiting each other every 104 days; the sixth-brightest star in the sky; a bright northern winter star

name: Rigel
notable_for: One of the most luminous stars known; a blue supergiant in the constellation Orion; the seventh-brightest star in the sky despite being nearly 1,000 light-years away; candidate future supernova

name: Procyon
notable_for: The eighth-brightest star in the sky; a subgiant beginning to evolve off the main sequence; has a white dwarf companion (Procyon B) that was predicted to exist before its discovery

name: Achernar
notable_for: The ninth-brightest star in the sky; one of the most oblate stars known — it spins so fast its equatorial radius is about 56% larger than its polar radius; the southern end of the river constellation Eridanus

name: Betelgeuse
notable_for: A red supergiant and one of the largest stars visible to the naked eye; will explode as a supernova within the next 100,000 years; experienced a 'Great Dimming' in 2019–2020 caused by dust ejection

name: Hadar
notable_for: Also called Agena; the 11th-brightest star in the sky; a triple star system consisting of two blue giants orbiting each other closely with a third companion; forms a well-known pointer to the Southern Cross with Alpha Centauri

name: Altair
notable_for: One of the vertices of the Summer Triangle; rotates so rapidly (once every 9 hours) that it is noticeably flattened at the poles; one of the closest naked-eye stars to Earth

name: Acrux
notable_for: The brightest star in the Southern Cross constellation and the 13th brightest in the sky; a multiple star system consisting of at least four components; appears on the flags of Australia, New Zealand, Brazil, and Papua New Guinea

name: Aldebaran
notable_for: The brightest star in Taurus; a red giant representing the eye of the bull; the Voyager 1 spacecraft is traveling in the general direction of Aldebaran and will reach its vicinity in 2 million years

name: Antares
notable_for: A red supergiant so large that if placed at the center of our Solar System, it would engulf Mars; its name means 'rival of Ares (Mars)' due to its reddish color; a prime candidate for a future supernova

name: Spica
notable_for: A binary star system so close together that both stars are distorted into egg shapes by mutual tidal forces; it helped Hipparchus discover the precession of the equinoxes in 127 BCE

name: Pollux
notable_for: The brightest star in Gemini despite its beta designation; the closest giant star to the Sun; host to a confirmed exoplanet (Pollux b or Thestias) discovered in 2006

name: Fomalhaut
notable_for: One of the Four Royal Stars of Persian astronomy; surrounded by a prominent circumstellar disk of dust; a candidate exoplanet (Fomalhaut b) was directly imaged by Hubble in 2008

name: Deneb
notable_for: One of the most intrinsically luminous stars known; forms one vertex of the Summer Triangle; despite being roughly 2,600 light-years away, it is still bright enough to be a first-magnitude star

name: Mimosa
notable_for: The second-brightest star in the Southern Cross; a spectroscopic binary; appears on the flags of Australia, New Zealand, Brazil, Samoa, and Papua New Guinea

name: Regulus
notable_for: The brightest star in Leo; one of the Four Royal Stars; rotates so fast (96% of the breakup velocity) that it is visibly oblate; part of a quadruple star system

name: Adhara
notable_for: The second-brightest star in Canis Major; was the brightest star in the sky around 4.7 million years ago due to its proper motion; the strongest known source of extreme ultraviolet radiation in the past

name: Shaula
notable_for: The second-brightest star in Scorpius, representing the stinger of the scorpion; a triple star system; one of the brightest ultraviolet stars in the sky

name: Castor
notable_for: Appears as a single bright star but is actually a sextuple star system (six stars total); the two brightest components are easily separated in a small telescope; named after one of the divine Gemini twins

name: Gacrux
notable_for: The third-brightest star in the Southern Cross and the top star of the cross; the nearest red giant to Earth; together with Acrux it points toward the south celestial pole

name: Bellatrix
notable_for: The third-brightest star in Orion; sometimes called the 'Amazon Star'; likely already evolved off the main sequence and beginning to cool; shares its name with a character in the Harry Potter series

name: El Nath
notable_for: The second-brightest star in Taurus, marking the tip of the northern horn of the Bull; was formerly also designated Gamma Aurigae as it lies on the border of Auriga; a blue giant star

name: Miaplacidus
notable_for: The second-brightest star in Carina and one of the navigational stars used in celestial navigation; its name is of mixed Arabic and Latin origin meaning 'placid waters'

name: Alnilam
notable_for: The middle star of Orion's Belt; one of the most luminous stars known; despite being over 1,300 light-years away, it is the 29th brightest star in the night sky; a massive blue supergiant losing mass rapidly

name: Gamma Velorum
notable_for: The nearest Wolf-Rayet star to Earth and the brightest in the sky; a massive multiple star system; the Wolf-Rayet component is losing mass at an enormous rate and will eventually explode as a supernova
